name: Lake Michigan (IN)
description:
Indiana's 45-mile Lake Michigan shoreline provides access to one of the Great Lakes' finest fisheries. The southern basin's relatively shallow, warm water attracts baitfish concentrations that draw predators close to shore.

Chinook salmon exceeding 30 pounds are the premier target during summer and fall, with Michigan City and the Indiana Dunes area serving as prime launch points. Perch fishing in spring and fall provides excellent action. Trail Creek and the harbor at Michigan City offer the best-developed launch facilities.

name: Monroe Lake
description:
Monroe Lake is Indiana's largest reservoir, located in the rolling hills of south-central Indiana near Bloomington and Indiana University. The 10,750-acre lake in the Hoosier National Forest offers diverse fishing opportunities.

Largemouth bass fishing is the primary draw, with the lake producing quality fish in the flooded timber and brush. Crappie fishing is exceptional during spring, when fish move shallow around standing timber. The lake also supports a stocked striped bass population that provides exciting open-water action. Multiple ramps provide access around the lake.

name: Patoka Lake
description:
Patoka Lake is Indiana's second-largest reservoir, nestled in the scenic hills of southern Indiana. The lake's extensive shoreline and numerous coves create excellent fish habitat throughout its winding arms.

Largemouth bass and crappie are the primary targets, with the flooded timber in the upper arms providing exceptional cover. Walleye stocking has created a growing fishery that produces fish over 5 pounds. The remote southern Indiana location means less fishing pressure than comparable lakes closer to Indianapolis.

name: Brookville Lake
description:
Brookville Lake is one of Indiana's premier walleye fisheries, a deep, clear reservoir in the Whitewater River valley of southeastern Indiana. The lake's depth and clarity set it apart from the typically stained waters of most Indiana reservoirs.

Walleye and saugeye are the signature species, with the annual spring run concentrating fish near the dam. Crappie fishing is outstanding in the creek arms during spring, and largemouth bass grow to impressive sizes in the clearer water. Multiple Army Corps of Engineers ramps provide access around the lake.

name: Mississinewa Lake
description:
Mississinewa Lake is a flood-control reservoir in north-central Indiana offering a diverse multi-species fishery. The lake's mix of shallow flats, creek channels, and deeper water near the dam supports a wide variety of game fish.

Musky stocking has created an emerging trophy fishery, with fish exceeding 40 inches reported. Largemouth bass and crappie provide the most consistent action, while saugeye fishing is best during spring and fall. The lake's proximity to Marion and Peru makes it a convenient destination for central Indiana anglers.

name: Salamonie Lake
description:
Salamonie Lake is a U.S. Army Corps of Engineers reservoir in northeastern Indiana, known for its outstanding walleye and saugeye fishing. The lake's relatively deep, clear water provides ideal habitat for cool-water species.

Spring walleye fishing near the dam is the highlight, with fish staging in the deeper water. Crappie fishing in the upper arms produces excellent catches during the spawn. The lake's wooded shoreline and rolling terrain make it one of Indiana's more scenic reservoirs. Multiple ramps provide access throughout the lake.

name: Lake Freeman
description:
Lake Freeman is one of the twin Tippecanoe River reservoirs near Monticello in north-central Indiana. The lake offers a mix of residential development and natural shoreline, creating diverse fishing opportunities.

Largemouth bass fishing is productive around the many docks and seawalls. Striped bass have been stocked and provide exciting open-water fishing. Crappie congregate around brush piles and bridge pilings. The connection to Lake Shafer downstream creates an extended fishing corridor. Several public ramps serve the lake.

name: Lake Shafer
description:
Lake Shafer sits upstream from Lake Freeman on the Tippecanoe River near Monticello, home to the Indiana Beach amusement park. The lake's channel-like shape along the river provides diverse habitat with current areas and backwater coves.

Walleye fishing is productive near the dam and in the river channel, especially during spring. Largemouth bass inhabit the docks and weed beds along the shoreline, while crappie stack up around submerged brush. The lake's connection to Lake Freeman and proximity to Indiana Beach make it a popular summer destination.
